planting.note: Scatter seed on bare or lightly raked soil in fall; seeds require cold winter temperatures to break dormancy and will germinate naturally in early spring. No covering is needed — simply press seed into contact with the soil surface.

directions: Western Tansy Mustard is a native annual of dry, open, sandy or rocky ground across the western US and Great Plains, producing finely pinnate, fern-like leaves with a pleasant aroma and dense racemes of small, pale yellow, 4-petaled mustard flowers from March through June. The cylindrical seed pods that follow are eagerly eaten by mourning doves, quail, and native sparrows, making it a high-value plant for bird-friendly gardens and prairie restorations. Direct sow in fall into lean, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; it thrives in poor soils where competition from other plants is minimal. It self-sows prolifically in open, disturbed ground and will naturalize a site with little intervention once established.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall; seeds will overwinter and germinate in early spring as soon as temperatures begin to rise. For an edible leaf crop, thin to 4-6 inches and harvest young basal leaves before the flower stalk rises.

directions: Virginia Peppergrass is a native annual or biennial of open, disturbed ground, roadsides, and garden edges throughout eastern North America, producing a basal rosette of toothed, peppery-flavored leaves followed by upright racemes of tiny white flowers and distinctive round, notched, flattened seed pods that rattle pleasingly in the breeze. The young basal leaves are edible raw in salads, contributing a sharp, peppery, radish-like heat, and the ripe seed pods can be ground or crushed as a mild pepper substitute in cooking. Sow directly in fall in thin, well-drained, even nutrient-poor soil in full sun; it germinates readily in disturbed or compacted ground where other plants struggle. Once established it self-sows freely, naturalizing open areas with minimal effort.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, shaded, rocky or sandy soil; cold winter temperatures provide the stratification needed to break seed dormancy. Plants form a basal rosette in the first year and bloom in their second spring.

directions: Smooth Rock Cress is a native biennial or short-lived perennial of moist, shaded rocky woodland slopes, bluff bases, and cliff ledges throughout eastern North America, quietly filling in some of the season's earliest wildflower gaps. In its first year it forms a flat, silvery-green basal rosette of smooth, clasping leaves; in its second spring it sends up a slender, upright stem bearing small, white to pale lavender, 4-petaled mustard flowers that attract early-season bees and native insects. The smooth, glossy stem leaves clasp the stalk with distinctive ear-like auricles that make identification easy. Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic, rocky or sandy, well-drained soil under partial shade; it naturalizes steadily in woodland borders by self-sowing, gradually colonizing rocky slopes and ledges with little intervention.
sources: Flora of North America - Boechera laevigata

planting.note: Sow on bare limestone-derived soil in fall; seeds require cold stratification over winter to germinate in late winter or early spring. Do not add organic matter or compost — this species demands thin, nutrient-poor, calcareous substrate.

directions: Michaux's Gladecress is a tiny, jewel-like native winter annual of limestone cedar glades in Tennessee, Kentucky, and adjacent states, one of the earliest wildflowers to bloom in the entire eastern flora, opening its single white 4-petaled flowers with yellow centers on thread-like stems as early as February through April. It completes its entire life cycle on the exposed, thin-soiled faces of limestone outcrops that experience brief seasonal saturation followed by intense summer drought — conditions nearly impossible to recreate in conventional gardens. Grow in thin, rocky, calcareous, extremely well-drained soil in full sun, deliberately avoiding soil amendments or fertilizer that would favor competing vegetation. It is an ecological specialist of rare, imperiled cedar glade habitat and is best suited for limestone glade restoration projects rather than traditional garden settings.

planting.note: Sow on bare, thin limestone-derived soil in fall for winter stratification and late-winter germination. This species requires extremely lean, calcareous, open substrate free of competing vegetation; any enrichment of the soil will favor weedy competitors over this diminutive specialist.

directions: Necklace Gladecress is a rare, federally listed native winter annual endemic to limestone cedar glades of central Tennessee and a handful of Kentucky sites, named for the striking bead-like constrictions between the seed compartments of its elongated seed pods that resemble a string of tiny pearls. Like its close relative Michaux's Gladecress, it produces small, white 4-petaled flowers on thread-like stems in late winter and early spring, completing its life cycle on exposed limestone rock faces before summer drought sets in. Grow exclusively in thin, limestone-derived, extremely well-drained, calcareous soil in full sun with no organic amendments; it cannot compete against taller vegetation and must be sited on open, bare rock surfaces or gravel substrate. Essentially unavailable through commercial horticulture, it is planted only in cedar glade conservation restorations — do not collect seed or plants from wild populations.
sources: Tennessee Department of Environment and Conservation - Rare Plant Program
